classification.cautionReason: No major mainstream heresy warning is attached by the preview classifier.

title: Homily LI on Acts xxiv. 22, 23.

source.title: NPNF1-11. Saint Chrysostom: Homilies on the Acts of the Apostles and the Epistle to the Romans

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And when Felix heard these things, having more perfect knowledge of that way, he deferred them and said, When Lysias the tribune shall come down, I will know the uttermost of your matter.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And he commanded a centurion to keep Paul, and to let him have liberty, and that he should forbid none of his acquaintance to minister or come unto him.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: See how much close investigation is made by the many in a long course of time, that it should not be said that the trial was hurried over.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For, as the orator had made mention of Lysias, that he took “him away with violence, Felix,” he says, “deferred them.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Having knowledge of that way:” that is, he put them off on purpose: not because he wanted to learn, but as wishing to get rid of the Jews.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: On their account, he did not like to let him go: to punish him was not possible; that would have been (too) barefaced.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And to let him have liberty, and to forbid none of his acquaintance to minister to him.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: So entirely did he too acquit him of the charges.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Howbeit, to gratify them, he detained him, and besides, expecting to receive money, he called for Paul.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And after certain days, when Felix came with his wife Drusilla, which was a Jewess, he sent for Paul, and heard him concerning the faith in Christ.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And as he reasoned of righteousness, temperance (i.e. self-control or chastity), and judgment to come, Felix trembled, and answered, Go thy way for this time; when I have a convenient season, I will call for thee.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: He hoped also that money should have been given him of Paul, that he might loose him; wherefore he sent for him the oftener, and communed with him.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But after two years Porcius Festus came into Felix’s room: and Felix, willing to show the Jews a pleasure, left Paul bound.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: See how close to the truth are the things written.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But he sent for him frequently, not that he admired him, nor that he praised the things spoken, nor that he wished to believe, but why?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Expecting,” it says, “that money should have been given him.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Observe how he does not hide here the mind of the judge.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Wherefore he sent for him,” etc. And yet if he had condemned him, he would not have done this, nor have wished to hear a man, condemned and of evil character.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And observe Paul, how, though reasoning with a ruler, he says nothing of the sort that was likely to amuse and entertain, but (“he reasoned,” it says,) “of righteousness, and of the coming judgment,” and of the resurrection.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And such was the force of his words, that they even terrified the governor.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: This man is succeeded in his office by another, and he leaves Paul a prisoner: and yet he ought not to have done this; he ought to have put an end to the business: but he leaves him, by way of gratifying them.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: They however were so urgent, that they again besought the judge.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Yet against none of the Apostles had they set themselves thus pertinaciously; there, when they had attacked, anon they desisted.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: So providentially is he removed from Jerusalem, having to do with such wild beasts.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And they nevertheless request that he might be brought again there to be tried.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Now when Festus was come into the province, after three days he ascended from Cæsarea to Jerusalem.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Then the high priest and the chief of the Jews informed him against Paul, and besought him, and desired favor against him, that he would send for him to Jerusalem, laying wait in the way to kill him.” (ch. xxv.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Here now God’s providence interposed, not permitting the governor to do this: for it was natural that he having just come to the government would wish to gratify them: but God suffered him not.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “But Festus answered, that Paul should be kept at Cæsarea, and that he himself would depart shortly thither.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Let them therefore, said he, which among you are able, go down with me, and accuse this man, if there be any wickedness in him.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And when he had tarried among them more than ten days, he went down unto Cæsarea; and the next day sitting on the judgment seat commanded Paul to be brought.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But after they came down, they forthwith made their accusations shamelessly and with more vehemence: and not having been able to convict him on grounds relating to the Law, they again according to their custom stirred the question about Cæsar, being just what they did in Christ’s case.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For that they had recourse to this is manifest by the fact, that Paul defends himself on the score of offences against Cæsar.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And when he was come, the Jews which came down from Jerusalem stood round about, and laid many and grievous complaints against Paul, which they could not prove.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: While he answered for himself, Neither against the law of the Jews, neither against the temple, nor yet against Cæsar, have I offended anything at all.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But Festus, willing to do the Jews a pleasure, answered Paul, and said, Wilt thou go up to Jerusalem, and there be judged of these things before me”? (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Wherefore he too gratifies the Jews, the whole people, and the city.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Such being the case, Paul terrifies him also, using a human weapon for his defence.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Then said Paul, I stand at Cæsar’s judgment seat, where I ought to be judged; to the Jews have I done no wrong, as thou very well knowest.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For if I be an offender, or have committed anything worthy of death, I refuse not to die: but if there be none of these things whereof these accuse me, no man may deliver me unto them.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: I appeal unto Cæsar.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Some one might say, How is it, that having been told, “Thou must also bear witness of Me in Rome,” (ch. xxiii.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 11), he, as if unbelieving, did this?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: God forbid: nay, he did it, because he so strongly believed.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For it would have been a tempting of God to be bold on account of that declaration, and to cast himself into numberless dangers, and to say: “Let us see if God is able even thus to deliver me.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But not so does Paul; no, he does his part, all that in him lies, committing the whole to God.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Quietly also he reproves the governor: for, “If, says he, I am an offender, thou doest well: but if not, why dost thou give me up?”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “No man,” he says, “may sacrifice me.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: He put him in fear, so that even if he wished, he could not sacrifice him to them; while also as an excuse to them he had Paul’s appeal to allege.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Then Festus, when he had conferred with the council, answered, Hast thou appealed unto Cæsar? unto Cæsar shalt thou go.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And after certain days king Agrippa and Bernice came unto Cæsarea to salute Festus.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Observe, he communicates the matter to Agrippa, so that there should be other hearers once more, both the king, and the army, and Bernice.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Thereupon a speech in his exculpation.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And when they had been there many days, Festus declared Paul’s cause unto the king, saying, There is a certain man left in bonds by Felix: about whom, when I was at Jerusalem, the chief priests and the elders of the Jews informed me, desiring to have judgment against him.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: To whom I answered, It is not the manner of the Romans to deliver any man to die, before that he which is accused have the accusers face to face, and have license to answer for himself concerning the crime laid against him.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Therefore, when they were come hither, without any delay on the morrow I sat on the judgment seat, and commanded the man to be brought forth.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Against whom when the accusers stood up, they brought none accusation of such things as I supposed: but had certain questions against him of their own superstition, and of one Jesus, which was dead, whom Paul affirmed to be alive.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And because I doubted of such manner of questions, I asked him whether he would go to Jerusalem, and there be judged of these matters.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But when Paul had appealed to be reserved unto the hearing of Augustus, I commanded him to be kept till I might send him to Cæsar.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Then Agrippa said unto Festus, I would also hear the man myself.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Tomorrow, said he, thou shalt hear him.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And observe a crimination of the Jews, not from Paul, but also from the governor.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Desiring,” he says, “to have judgment against him.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: To whom I said, to their shame, that “it is not the manner of the Romans,” before giving an opportunity to speak for himself, “to sacrifice a man.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But I did give him (such opportunity), and I found no fault in him.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Because I doubted,” says he, of “such manner of questions:” he casts a veil also over his own wrong.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Then the other desires to see him. (b) But let us look again at what has been said.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: (Recapitulation.)

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And when Felix,” etc. (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Observe on all occasions how the governors try to keep off from themselves the annoyance of the Jews, and are often compelled to act contrary to justice, and seek pretexts for deferring: for of course it was not from ignorance that he deferred the cause, but knowing it.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And his wife also hears, together with the governor. (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: This seems to me to show great honor.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For he would not have brought his wife to be present with him at the hearing, but that he thought great things of him.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: It seems to me that she also longed for this.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And observe how Paul immediately discourses not only about faith, nor about remission of sins, but also about practical points of duty.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Go thy way,” he says, “for this time: when I have a convenient season, I will call for thee.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Observe his hardness of heart: hearing such things, “he hoped that he should receive money from him!” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And not only so, but even after conversing with him—for it was towards the end of his government—he left him bound, “willing to show the Jews a pleasure” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 27): so that he not only coveted money, but also glory.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: How, O wretch, canst thou look for money from a man who preaches the contrary?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But that he did not get it, is evident from his leaving him bound; he would have loosed him, had he received it.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Of temperance,” it says, he reasoned; but the other was hankering to receive money from him who discoursed these things!

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And to ask indeed he did not dare: for such is wickedness: but he hoped it.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And when two years were completed,” etc., so that it was but natural that he showed them a pleasure, as he had been so long governor there.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Now when Festus was come into the province,” etc. (ch. xxv.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: At the very beginning, the priests came to him, who would not have hesitated to go even to Cæsarea, unless he had been seen immediately coming up, since immediately on his arrival they come to him.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And he spends ten days, in order, I suppose, to be open to those who wished to corrupt him with bribes.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But Paul was in the prison.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “They besought him,” it says, “that he would send for him:” why did they desire it as a favor, if he was deserving of death?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But thus their plotting became evident even to him, so that discoursing of it (to Agrippa), he says, “desiring to have judgment against him.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: They wanted to induce him to pass sentence now immediately, being afraid of Paul’s tongue.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: What are ye afraid of?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: What are ye in such a hurry?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: In fact, that expression, “that he should be kept” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 4), shows this.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Does he want to escape?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Let them therefore,” he says, “which among you are able, accuse him.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Again accusers, again at Cæsarea, again Paul is brought forth.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And having come, immediately “he sat on the judgment-seat” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 6); with all his haste: they so drove, so hurried him.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: While as yet he had not got acquainted with the Jews, nor experienced the honor paid to him by them, he answered rightly: but now that he had been in Jerusalem ten days, he too wants to pleasure them (by sacrificing Paul to them): then, also to receive Paul, “Wilt thou,” says he, “be judged there of these things by me?” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: I am not giving thee up to them—but this was the fact—and he leaves the point to his own choice, that by this mark of respect he might get him to yield: since his was the sentence, and it would have been too barefaced, when he had been convicted of nothing here, to take him back thither.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “But Paul said, At Cæsar’s tribunal am I standing,” etc. (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 10): he did not say, I will not, lest he should make the judge more vehement, but (here) again is his great boldness: They cast me out once for all, themselves, and by this they think to condemn me, by their showing that I have offended against Cæsar: at his bar I choose to be judged, at the bar of the injured person himself.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “To the Jews have I done no wrong, as thou also very well knowest.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Here now he reproved him, that he too wished to sacrifice him to the Jews: then, on the other hand, he relaxes (the sternness of) his speech: “if then I be an offender, or have committed anything worthy of death, I refuse not to die.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: I utter sentence against myself.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For along with boldness of speech there must be also justness of cause, so as to abash (the hearer).

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “But if there be nothing in the things whereof these accuse me, no man”—however he may wish it—“no man may sacrifice me to please them.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: He said, not, I am not worthy of death, nor, I am worthy to be acquitted, but, I am ready to take my trial before Cæsar.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: At the same time too, remembering the dream, he was the more confident to appeal. (ch. xxiii.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 11) And he said not, Thou (mayest not), but, neither any other man may sacrifice me, that it might be no affront to him.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Then Festus, when he had conferred with the council”—do you observe how he seeks to gratify them? for this is favor—“having conferred,” it says, “with the council, he said, Hast thou appealed unto Cæsar? unto Cæsar shalt thou go.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: See how his trial is again lengthened out, and how the plot against him becomes an occasion for the preaching: so that with ease and in safe custody he should be taken away to Rome, with none to plot evil against him: for it was not the same thing his simply coming there, and his coming on such a cause.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For, in fact this was what made the Jews come together there. (ch. xxviii.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Then again, some time passes while he tarries at Jerusalem, that you may learn, that, though some time passed, the evil design against him prevails nothing, God not permitting it.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But this king Agrippa, who was also a Herod, was a different Agrippa, after him of James’ time, so that this is the fourth (Herod).

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: See how his enemies coöperate with him against their will.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: To make the audience large, Agrippa falls into a desire of hearing: and he does not simply hear, but with much parade.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And see what a vindication (απολογιαν)!

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: So writes Festus, and the ruthlessness of the Jews is openly made a show of: for when it is the governor that says these things, he is a witness above all suspicion: so that the Jews are condemned by him also.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For, when all had pronounced sentence against them, then, and not sooner, God brings upon them the punishment.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But observe: Lysias gave it against them, Felix against them, Festus against them—although he wished to gratify them—Agrippa against them.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: What further?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: The Pharisees—even they gave it against themselves.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: No evil, says Festus, “of such things as I supposed: no accusation did they bring against him.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And yet they did bring it: true, but they did not prove it: for their evil design and daring plot against him gave cause to surmise this, but the examination brought out nothing of the kind.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And of one Jesus,” he says, “which was dead.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: He says naturally enough, “of one” (Jesus), as being a man in office, and not caring for these things.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And not knowing, for my part, what to make of the enquiry concerning these things” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 20)—of course, it went beyond a judge’s hearing, the examining into these matters.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: If thou art at a loss, why dost thou drag him to Jerusalem?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But the other would not deign this: no, “To Cæsar” (says he); as in fact it was touching Cæsar that they accused him.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Do you hear the appeal? hear the plotting of the Jews? hear their factious spirit?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: All these things provoked him to a desire (of hearing him): and he gives them the gratification and Paul becomes more renowned.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For such as I said, are the ill designs (of enemies).

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Had not these things been so, none of these rulers would have deigned to hear him, none would have heard with such quietness and silence.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And he seems indeed to be teaching, he seems to be making a defence; but he rather makes a public harangue with much orderliness.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Then let us not think that ill designs against us are a grievous thing.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: So long as we do not make ill designs against ourselves, no one will be able to have ill designs against us: or rather, people may do this, but they do us no hurt; nay, even benefit us in the highest degree: for it rests with ourselves, whether we shall suffer evil, or not suffer evil.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: I testify, and proclaim with a loud voice, more piercing even than the sound of a trumpet—and were it possible to ascend on high and cry aloud, I would not shrink from doing it—him that is a Christian, none of all the human beings that inhabit the earth will have power to hurt.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And why do I say, human beings?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Not even the Evil Spirit himself, the tyrant, the Devil, can do this, unless the man injure himself; be what it may that any one works, in vain he works it.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For even as no human being could hurt an angel, if he were on earth, so neither can one human being hurt another human being.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But neither again will he himself be able to hurt another, so long as he is good.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: What then can be equal to this, when neither to be hurt is possible, nor to hurt another?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For this thing is not less than the former, the not wishing to hurt another.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Why, that man is a kind of angel, yea, like God.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For such is God; only, He indeed (is such) by nature, but this man, by moral choice: neither to be hurt is possible (for either), nor to hurt another.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But this thing, this “not possible,” think not that it is for any want of power—for the contrary to this is want of power—no, I speak of the morally incompatible (τὸ ἀνενδεκτόν).

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For the (Divine) Nature is neither Itself susceptible of hurt, nor capable of hurting another: since this very thing in itself is a hurt.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For in no other way do we hurt ourselves, than by hurting another, and our greatest sins become such from our doing injury to ourselves.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: So that for this reason also the Christian cannot be hurt, namely, because neither can he hurt.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But how in hurting others we hurt ourselves, come, let us take this saying in hand for examination in detail.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Let a man wrong another, insult, overreach; whom then has he hurt?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Is it not himself first?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: This is plain to every one.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For to the one, the damage is in money, to himself, it is in the soul; to destruction, and to punishment.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Again, let another be envious: is it not himself he has injured?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For such is the nature of injustice: to its own author first it does incalculable hurt.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Yes, but to another also?”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: True, but nothing worth considering: or rather, not even a little—nay, it even benefits him.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For let there be,—as the whole matter lies most in these examples,—let there be some poor man, having but little property and (barely) provided with necessary food, and another rich and wealthy, and having much power, and then let him take the poor man’s property, and strip him naked, and give him up to starvation, while he shall luxuriate in what he has unjustly taken from the other: not only has he not hurt that man at all—he has even benefited him, while himself he has not only not benefited, but even hurt.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For how should it be otherwise?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: In the first place, harassed by an evil conscience, and day by day condemning himself and being condemned by all men: and then, secondly, in the judgment to come.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But the other, how is he benefited?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Because to suffer ill and bear it nobly, is great gain: for it is a doing away of sins, this suffering of ill, it is a training to philosophy, it is a discipline of virtue.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Let us see which of the two is in evil case, this man or that.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For the one, if he be a man of well-ordered mind, will bear it nobly: the other will be every day in a constant tremor and misgiving: which then is hurt, this man or that?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “You talk idly,” say you: “for when a man has nothing to eat, and is forced to bewail himself and to feel himself very wretched, or comes and begs, and gets nothing, is not that a ruining of both soul and body?”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: No, it is you that talk idly: for I show facts in proof.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For say, does none of the rich feel himself wretched?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Is poverty the cause of his wretchedness?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “But he does not starve.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And what of that?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: The greater is the punishment, when having riches he does this.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For neither does wealth make a man strong-minded, nor poverty make him weak: otherwise none of those living in wealth would pass a wretched life, nor would any of those in poverty (not) curse his fate.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But that yours is indeed the idle talk, I will make manifest to you from hence.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Was Paul in poverty or in wealth? did he suffer hunger, or did he not?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: You may hear himself saying, “In hunger and thirst.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Did the prophets suffer hunger, or did they not?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: They too had a hard time of it.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Again, you fetch up Paul to me, again the prophets, some ten or twenty men.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But whence shall I bring examples?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Show me from the many some who bear ills nobly.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But the rare is ever such: however, if you will, let us examine the matter as it is in itself.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Let us see whose is the greater and sharper care, whose the more easy to be borne.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: The one is solicitous about his necessary food, the other about numberless matters, freed from that care.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: The rich man is not afraid on the score of hunger, but he is afraid about other things: oftentimes for his very life.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: The poor man is not free from anxiety about food, but he is free from other anxieties, he has safety, has quietness, has security.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: If to injure another is not an evil, but a good, wherefore are we ashamed? wherefore do we cover our faces?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Wherefore, being reproached, are we vexed and disconcerted?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: If the being injured is not a good thing, wherefore do we pride ourselves, and glory in the thing, and justify ourselves on its account?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Would you learn how this is better than that?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Observe those who are in the one condition, and those who are in the other.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Wherefore are laws?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Wherefore are courts of justice?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Wherefore punishments?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Is it not, on account of those men, as being diseased and unsound?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But the pleasure lies great, you will say.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Let us not speak of the future: let us look into the present.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: What is worse than a man who is under such a suspicion as this? what more precarious? what more unsound? is he not always in a state of shipwreck?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Even if he do any just thing, he is not credited, condemned as he is by all on account of his power (of injuring): for in all who dwell with him he has accusers: he cannot enjoy friendship: for none would readily choose to become the friend of a man who has such a character, for fear of becoming implicated with him in the opinion held of him.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: As if he were a wild beast, all men turn away from him; as from a pest, a foe, a man-slayer, and an enemy of nature, so they shrink from the unjust man.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: If he who has wronged another happen to be brought into a court of justice, he does not even need an accuser, his character condemns him in place of any accuser.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Not so he who is injured; he has all men to take his part, to condole with him, to stretch out the hand of help: he stands on safe ground.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: If to injure another be a good and a safe thing, let any one confess that he is unjust: but if he dares not do this, why then does he pursue it as a good thing?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But let us see in our own persons, if his same be done there, what evils come of it: (I mean,) if any of the parts or functions within us having overstepped its proper bounds, grasp at the office of some other.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For let the spleen, if it will, have left its proper place, and seize on the part belonging to some other organ along with its own, is not this disease?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: The moisture within us, let it fill every place, is it not dropsy and gout? is not this to ruin itself, along with the other?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Again, let the bile seek for a wide room, and let the blood be diffused throughout every part.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But how is it in the soul with anger, lust, and all the rest, if the food exceed its proper measure?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Again in the body, if the eye wish to take in more, or to see more than is allotted to it, or admit a greater light than is proper.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But if, when the light is good, yet the eye is ruined, if it choose to see more than is right: consider what it must be in the case of an evil thing.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: If the ear take in a (too) loud voice, the sense is stunned: the mind, if it reason about things above itself, it is overpowered: and whatever is in excess, mars all.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For this is πλεονεξία, the wanting to have more than what is marked off and allotted.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: So too in respect of money; when we will needs put upon (us) more burdens (than is meet), although we do not perceive it, to our sore hurt we are nourishing within ourselves a wild beast; much having, yet much wanting, numberless the cares we entangle ourselves withal, many the handles we furnish the devil against ourselves.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: In the case of the rich, however, the devil has not even need of labor, so surely do their very concerns of business of themselves ruin them.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Wherefore I beseech you to abstain from the lust of these things, that we may be enabled to escape the snares of the evil one, and having taken hold of virtue, to attain unto the good things eternal, through the grace and mercy of our Lord Jesus Christ, with Whom to the Father and the Holy Ghost together be glory forever.
